Done — `python3 -m pytest -q` now passes all 6 tests. There were two bugs in `lrucache/cache.py`:

1. **`get` didn't refresh recency** (`cache.py:30`). A hit returned the value without moving the key to the most-recently-used end of the `OrderedDict`, so reading an entry didn't protect it from eviction — the next `put` evicted it anyway. Fixed by adding `move_to_end(key)` on a non-expired hit.

2. **`put` evicted even when updating an existing key** (`cache.py:33`). The eviction check only looked at `len >= capacity`, so overwriting a key in a full cache wrongly kicked out the least-recently-used entry. Fixed by evicting only when the key is new and the cache is at capacity.

`SOLUTION.md` is written at the repo root with the same explanation, and nothing under `tests/` was touched.

All 6 tests pass. There were two bugs, both in `lrucache/cache.py`:

1. **`get` didn't refresh recency** — a hit returned the value without `move_to_end(key)`, so a freshly read entry could still be evicted as the "least recently used". Added the `move_to_end` call on non-expired hits (`cache.py:30`).
2. **`put` evicted on in-place updates** — the eviction check ran even when the key already existed, so overwriting a key in a full cache wrongly evicted another entry. Now it only evicts when inserting a genuinely new key (`cache.py:34`).

`SOLUTION.md` is written with the same explanation, and `python3 -m pytest -q` reports `6 passed`.

2. **`put` evicted when updating an existing key** — the eviction check fired whenever the cache was at capacity, even when overwriting a key already present (which doesn't grow the cache). That wrongly evicted an unrelated entry. Fixed by only evicting when the key is new (`lrucache/cache.py:34`).

Two bugs were fixed in `lrucache/cache.py`:

2. **`put` evicted on in-place updates** — the eviction ran whenever the cache was full, even when the key already existed (so no growth). Now it only evicts when the key isn't already present.

Both bugs are fixed and the full suite passes (`6 passed`). Summary of what was wrong in `lrucache/cache.py`:

1. **`get` didn't refresh recency** — despite the docstring saying reads count as a "use", `get` never moved the accessed key to the end of the `OrderedDict`. A just-read key stayed at the LRU end and got wrongly evicted on the next `put`. Fixed by adding `move_to_end` on successful reads.

2. **`put` evicted on in-place updates** — the eviction check ran unconditionally, so updating an existing key while at capacity evicted an unrelated entry. Fixed by evicting only when the key is new (`key not in self._data`).

I wrote `SOLUTION.md` documenting both root causes, and did not touch anything under `tests/`.

All five functions are implemented in `problems.py` and every check passes, including a brute-force cross-check and a 1M-element performance run.

How each is implemented:

- **`parse_duration`** — matched against the anchored regex `(?:([0-9]+)h)?(?:([0-9]+)m)?(?:([0-9]+)s)?` via `fullmatch`, which structurally rejects out-of-order components, duplicates, bare units, negatives, whitespace, and trailing garbage. I used `[0-9]` instead of `\d` so Unicode digits are rejected too. Non-strings and the all-empty match (at least one component required) raise `ValueError`.
- **`luhn_valid`** — strips spaces/hyphens, returns `False` for empty, non-digit, or non-string input, then runs the standard right-to-left double-every-second-digit checksum. `"0"` passes, every other single digit fails.
- **`flatten`** — recursive walk building joined keys with `sep`; empty nested dicts contribute nothing, non-dict values (lists included) pass through untouched, and the input is never mutated.
- **`top_k_frequent`** — `Counter` plus a sort keyed on `(-count, word)` for the frequency-then-alphabetical order; returns `[]` for `k <= 0` and truncates with `[:k]` (which naturally handles `k` exceeding the distinct count).
- **`rolling_max`** — monotonic deque of indices (O(n)); raises `ValueError` for `window <= 0`, returns `[]` when the window exceeds the input length. The 1M-element, window-5000 run finished in 0.39s.

My throwaway checks covered all the docstring edge cases (e.g. `"30m1h"`, `"1h2h"`, `"h"`, whitespace variants, the classic Luhn number `79927398713`, `flatten({"a": {}})`, tie-breaking, and 300 randomized `rolling_max` comparisons against a naive `max()` implementation). No extra files were left behind — the check script ran inline via stdin.

## Data-loss and correctness bugs

**Pagination floor division (`src/sync.py:14`).** `for page in range(total_count // PAGE_SIZE)` uses floor division, so it only fetches whole pages. With `total_count=250` and `PAGE_SIZE=100` it fetches 2 pages = 200 rows, i.e. 200/250 = **80% fetched, 20% (50 rows) silently dropped**. The edge case that matters most: any `total_count < 100` (e.g. 50 staged users) computes `50 // 100 = 0`, so **0% are fetched** — the function returns an empty list. The pre-change code (`SELECT ... FROM staged_users`) returned everything, so this is a pure regression.

**Mutable default argument (`src/sync.py:24`).** `seen=[]` is evaluated once at definition time and shared across calls. The first `sync_users` run populates it; a second run in the same process reuses the same list still holding the earlier ids, so **100% of previously-synced users are skipped** as bogus "duplicates." A dedupe intended to guard against duplicates *within* one batch instead silently no-ops repeat sync runs.

## Security / silent-failure

**SQL injection (`src/sync.py:35`).** The diff replaces a parameterized `UPDATE ... (email, id)` with an f-string. Beyond the injection vector (email is attacker-influenceable staging data), it also breaks on ordinary data: any email with an apostrophe like `o'brien@example.com` produces malformed SQL.

**Swallowed exceptions (`src/sync.py:38`).** The handler changed from `log.exception(...)` to `pass`.

These last two **compound**: an apostrophe email now throws a SQL syntax error that is silently caught, so the user is never synced and nothing is logged — silent data loss. It compounds a third way with the mutable-default bug: `seen.append(user["id"])` runs *before* the `try` (line 31 precedes line 32), so a user whose UPDATE fails is still marked seen, meaning even an in-process retry skips it permanently.

## Report bugs

**File-descriptor leak (`src/report.py:5`).** Switching from `with open(...)` to a bare `open()` with no try/finally means `f.close()` (line 13) is only reached on the happy path. The empty-`users` early return at line 7 leaks the handle after truncating the file.

**None/malformed email crash (`src/report.py:11`).** `user["email"].lower().split("@")[1]` raises `AttributeError` when email is `None` — which `src/db.py:3-5` explicitly documents as a valid state for phone-invited users — and `IndexError` for any email without `@`. The old code handled this with `user["email"] or "unverified"`.

These two also **compound**: because there is no `with`/try-finally, a single `None` email raises at line 11 *before* `f.close()`, so the report bug triggers the leak path even for a non-empty user list — one bad row both crashes the report and leaks the descriptor.
